label: Maintainability
summary: Commented-out code, sync/async confusion, and other upkeep signals.

rules.label: Broad Exception Handler
rules.description: Broad except Exception: style handlers that still obscure failure shape even when not fully swallowed.
rules.explanation: In Python, Broad Exception Handler matters because the pattern here, Broad except Exception: style handlers that still obscure failure shape even when not fully swallowed, blurs the boundary between request plumbing and the rest of the application.
rules.fix: For Broad Exception Handler in Python, move the non-transport work into middleware, services, bootstrap code, or shared helpers so the request edge stays narrow.

rules.label: Builtin Reduction Candidate
rules.description: Loop shapes that look like obvious sum, any, or all candidates.
rules.explanation: In Python, Builtin Reduction Candidate matters because the pattern here, Loop shapes that look like obvious sum, any, or all candidates, turns local work into repeated cost that spreads with traffic or input size.
rules.fix: For Builtin Reduction Candidate in Python, move one-time initialization, parsing, or allocation outside the loop when the per-iteration state does not actually change.

rules.label: Commented Out Code
rules.description: Blocks of commented-out source code left in production files.
rules.explanation: In Python, Commented Out Code matters because the pattern here, Blocks of commented-out source code left in production files, is worth review because cleanup debt compounds until every file carries a little uncertainty about what is still real.
rules.fix: For Commented Out Code in Python, delete dead code, keep async and sync boundaries explicit, and reduce shapes that future maintainers have to second-guess.

rules.label: Environment Boundary without Fallback
rules.description: Environment-variable lookups that omit a default value or explicit failure handler.
rules.explanation: In Python, Environment Boundary without Fallback matters because the pattern here, Environment-variable lookups that omit a default value or explicit failure handler, pushes configuration or startup ownership into the wrong phase of the program.
rules.fix: For Environment Boundary without Fallback in Python, load, validate, and wire this configuration at startup so request paths and imports only consume already-owned state.

rules.label: Eval Exec Usage
rules.description: Direct eval() or exec() usage in non-test Python code.
rules.explanation: In Python, Eval Exec Usage matters because the pattern here, Direct eval() or exec() usage in non-test Python code, weakens the evidence that tests or review are supposed to provide.
rules.fix: For Eval Exec Usage in Python, tighten the tests until the failure mode, edge case, or assertion signal is obvious to a reviewer.

rules.label: Exception Swallowed
rules.description: Broad exception handlers like except: or except Exception: that immediately suppress the error with pass, continue, break, or return.
rules.explanation: In Python, Exception Swallowed matters because the pattern here, Broad exception handlers like except: or except Exception: that immediately suppress the error with pass, continue, break, or return, is worth review because cleanup debt compounds until every file carries a little uncertainty about what is still real.
rules.fix: For Exception Swallowed in Python, delete dead code, keep async and sync boundaries explicit, and reduce shapes that future maintainers have to second-guess.

rules.label: External Input without Validation
rules.description: Request or CLI entry points that trust external input without obvious validation or guard checks.
rules.explanation: In Python, External Input without Validation matters because the pattern here, Request or CLI entry points that trust external input without obvious validation or guard checks, removes a guardrail or signal that reviewers expect before trusting the surrounding code.
rules.fix: For External Input without Validation in Python, add obvious validation or guard checks or move the code to a path where that safeguard is guaranteed before the risky work happens.

rules.label: Hardcoded Business Rule
rules.description: Hardcoded threshold, rate-limit, or pricing-style literals assigned inside non-test Python functions.
rules.explanation: In Python, Hardcoded Business Rule matters because the pattern here, Hardcoded threshold, rate-limit, or pricing-style literals assigned inside non-test Python functions, weakens the evidence that tests or review are supposed to provide.
rules.fix: For Hardcoded Business Rule in Python, tighten the tests until the failure mode, edge case, or assertion signal is obvious to a reviewer.

rules.label: Hardcoded Path String
rules.description: Hardcoded filesystem path literals assigned inside non-test Python functions.
rules.explanation: In Python, Hardcoded Path String matters because the pattern here, Hardcoded filesystem path literals assigned inside non-test Python functions, weakens the evidence that tests or review are supposed to provide.
rules.fix: For Hardcoded Path String in Python, prefer a builder, buffer, or one-time composition step instead of growing the same value incrementally.

rules.label: Magic Value Branching
rules.description: Repeated branch-shaping numeric or string literals that likely want an explicit constant or policy name.
rules.explanation: In Python, Magic Value Branching matters because the pattern here, Repeated branch-shaping numeric or string literals that likely want an explicit constant or policy name, hides the real job of the symbol behind wording that is harder to scan during review.
rules.fix: For Magic Value Branching in Python, rename the symbol so its domain job is obvious without reading the whole body.

rules.label: Missing Context Manager
rules.description: Resource management (files, network connections) inside non-test Python functions that omits with-statement context managers.
rules.explanation: In Python, Missing Context Manager matters because the pattern here, Resource management (files, network connections) inside non-test Python functions that omits with-statement context managers, creates lifecycle behavior that is easy to miss until the system is under load or shutting down.
rules.fix: For Missing Context Manager in Python, give the work an explicit owner, pass the right context or handle, and make cancellation or shutdown visible in the API.

rules.label: Mixed Sync Async Module
rules.description: Modules that expose public sync and async entry points together.
rules.explanation: In Python, Mixed Sync Async Module matters because the pattern here, Modules that expose public sync and async entry points together, makes ownership and navigation harder when someone has to trace behavior across modules or services.
rules.fix: For Mixed Sync Async Module in Python, move the code to the package, module, router, or entrypoint layer that already owns this boundary so navigation and responsibility stay aligned.

rules.label: Network Boundary without Timeout
rules.description: Request, sync, or job-style Python functions that call HTTP boundaries with no obvious timeout or retry policy.
rules.explanation: In Python, Network Boundary without Timeout matters because the pattern here, Request, sync, or job-style Python functions that call HTTP boundaries with no obvious timeout or retry policy, is worth review because cleanup debt compounds until every file carries a little uncertainty about what is still real.
rules.fix: For Network Boundary without Timeout in Python, delete dead code, keep async and sync boundaries explicit, and reduce shapes that future maintainers have to second-guess.

rules.label: None Comparison
rules.description: == None or != None checks instead of is None or is not None.
rules.explanation: In Python, None Comparison matters because the pattern here, == None or != None checks instead of is None or is not None, shows that the code is bypassing a clearer or safer default that readers usually expect to see.
rules.fix: For None Comparison in Python, prefer is None or is not None when the semantics match, and keep the expected or cheaper API choice as the default.

rules.label: Print Debugging Leftover

rules.fix: For Print Debugging Leftover in Python, move the code to the package, module, router, or entrypoint layer that already owns this boundary so navigation and responsibility stay aligned.

rules.label: Variadic Public API
rules.description: Public Python functions that expose *args or **kwargs instead of a clearer interface.
rules.explanation: In Python, Variadic Public API matters because the pattern here, Public Python functions that expose *args or **kwargs instead of a clearer interface, shows that the code is bypassing a clearer or safer default that readers usually expect to see.
rules.fix: For Variadic Public API in Python, prefer a clearer interface when the semantics match, and keep the expected or cheaper API choice as the default.
